title: Neurotoxicity of chronic low-dose exposure to organic solvents: A skeptical review
abstract:
Neurotoxicity of Chronic Low-Dose Exposure
to Organic Solvents: A Skeptical Review
Ä
Paul R. Lees–Haley and Christopher W. Williams
Lees–Haley Psychological Corporation
The health effects of long-term, low-level exposure to organic solvents
have been studied for many years. While the volume of literature is great,
definitive conclusions regarding chronic neurobehavioral effects of envi-
ronmental exposure are premature. Methodological shortcomings in
research preclude confidence in studies allegedly supporting a causal link
between chronic low-dose solvent exposure and lasting neurobehavioral
deficits. In this article, the shortcomings reviewed include selection bias in
recruitment of research subjects, overreliance on subjective recall in deter-
mining levels and duration of exposure, between-study variability in kinds
of solvents examined, variability in tests selected to assess neurobehav-
ioral functioning, and diversity in reported findings. The implications of
these for characterizing the state of organic solvent research are dis-
cussed. © 1997 John Wiley & Sons, Inc. J Clin Psychol 53: 699–712,
1997.
Organic solvents are a group of chemicals that are used for extracting, dissolving, or suspend-
ing materials not soluble in water. Most often they are encountered by people in occupations
such as housepainting, dry cleaning, machine degreasing, and printing. They can also be encoun-
tered in nonoccupational settings by homeowners, for example, when cleaning paintbrushes,
thinning paint, lacquering furniture, or by hobbyists when using certain glues for building
models. Because of heightened concern for the health of persons coming into contact regularly
with solvents, and because they provide an available subject pool, most research on the neuro-
toxic effects of solvent exposure has been conducted with persons in occupational settings.
Because of their chemical composition, excessive exposure to solvents can have depres-
sive effects on the central nervous system (CNS). Solvents are lipophilic or strongly attracted to
fats and have an affinity for lipid-rich tissues, including brain tissue, where they have been
implicated in producing a wide range of neurobehavioral symptoms. Highly concentrated acute
exposure can result in symptoms that range from lightheadedness to death. Animal and human
studies have documented these effects (Kulig, 1990).
Address correspondence to Paul R. Lees–Haley, 5445 Balboa Blvd., Suite 117, Encino, CA 91316.
JOURNAL OF CLINICAL PSYCHOLOGY, Vol. 53(7), 699–712 (1997)
© 1997 John Wiley & Sons, Inc.
CCC 0021-9762/97/070699-14
 However, the long-term effects of prolonged, low-level exposure to solvents are contro-
versial. Some studies have purported to document chronic effects in persons whose occupation
brings them into daily contact with solvents (Arlien–Soborg, Bruhn, Gyldensted, & Melgaard,
1979; Lindstrom, 1973; Mikkelsen, Jorgensen, Browne, & Gyldensted, 1988). Other studies
have failed to show such effects (Maizlish et al., 1985; Spurgeon, Glass, Calvert, Cunningham–
Hill, & Harrington, 1994; Triebig et al., 1988), or have reanalyzed data from earlier studies and
refuted claims of detrimental effects from sustained low-grade exposure (Gade, Mortensen, &
Bruhn, 1988; Grasso, Sharratt, Davies, & Irvine, 1984).
Some researchers argue that an emerging consensus supported by the cumulative weight of
evidence from over 20 years of research favors the view that prolonged exposure to organic
solvents causes the chronic and often irreversible neurobehavioral deficits labeled chronic toxic
encephalopathy (Baker, 1994). However, a careful examination of the methodology of the
studies commonly cited as a basis for this conclusion reveals methodological flaws sufficiently
serious to compel suspending judgment on this issue pending controlled studies with fewer
limitations.
The purpose of this article is to examine some of the methodological flaws of studies
supporting this conclusion. We argue that potential confounds and other factors preclude a
consensus on the chronic neurobehavioral effects of exposure to organic solvents because of
selection bias in recruitment of research subjects; overreliance on subject recall in determin-
ing levels and duration of exposure, assessment of health status and premorbid func-
tioning; between-study variability in tests used to assess neurobehavioral functioning and in the
results; between-study variability in the kinds of solvents examined; and lack of dose–response
documentation. These deficiencies, present in many studies, cast doubt on findings supporting
a causal link between continuous, low-level exposure to organic solvents and chronic toxic
encephalopathy.
INTRODUCTION
Many of the earlier studies were conducted in Scandinavian countries where a “painters’ syn-
drome” could lead to premature retirement and disability benefits. Painters and persons in other
trades would present themselves to clinics with symptoms of “organic solvent disease,” char-
acterized primarily by impairment of memory, lack of coordination, and some deterioration of
personality. The concern of these earlier studies was to document empirically the link between
exposure to organic solvents and behavioral deficits. One specific goal was to assemble a
battery of neuropsychological tests sensitive enough to detect reliably subclinical neurobehav-
ioral effects of exposure to neurotoxins (Hanninen, 1971; Ryan, Morrow, Bromet, & Parkinson,
1987; Russell, Flattau, & Pope, 1990). It was thought that a reliable test battery would be useful
for detecting problems early on before acute effects became chronic, providing a taxonomy of
neurotoxic symptoms, identifying individuals with legitimate complaints, providing guidance
to industry in adopting safety measures whereby employees could avoid overexposure, devel-
oping alternatives to solvents that were less toxic, and so on.
In one study (Gregersen, Mikkelsen, Klausen, Dossing, Neilsen, & Thygesen, 1978), a
“chronic cerebral painters’ syndrome” was reportedly found among a group of 35 painters
ranging in age from 27 to 58 years. A critical review of this research reported several errors in
methodology casting doubt on this finding (Errebo–Knudsen, & Olsen, 1986). Included in the
weakness was lack of a control group matched to the exposed group on potentially confounding
variables, factors related to the same kinds of symptoms or health problems as those linked
to solvents (e.g., alcohol use, health status, educational level, age). Absence of nonexposed
control groups matched to exposed individuals on critical variables has been a repeated criti-
700
Journal of Clinical Psychology, November 1997

Neurotoxicity of Chronic Low-Dose Exposure
to Organic Solvents: A Skeptical Review
Paul R. Lees–Haley and Christopher W. Williams
Lees–Haley Psychological Corporation

have been studied for many years. While the volume of literature is great,
definitive conclusions regarding chronic neurobehavioral effects of envi-
ronmental exposure are premature. Methodological shortcomings in
research preclude confidence in studies allegedly supporting a causal link
between chronic low-dose solvent exposure and lasting neurobehavioral
deficits. In this article, the shortcomings reviewed include selection bias in
recruitment of research subjects, overreliance on subjective recall in deter-
mining levels and duration of exposure, between-study variability in kinds
of solvents examined, variability in tests selected to assess neurobehav-
ioral functioning, and diversity in reported findings. The implications of
these for characterizing the state of organic solvent research are dis-
cussed. © 1997 John Wiley & Sons, Inc. J Clin Psychol 53: 699–712,

ing materials not soluble in water. Most often they are encountered by people in occupations
such as housepainting, dry cleaning, machine degreasing, and printing. They can also be encoun-
tered in nonoccupational settings by homeowners, for example, when cleaning paintbrushes,
thinning paint, lacquering furniture, or by hobbyists when using certain glues for building
models. Because of heightened concern for the health of persons coming into contact regularly
with solvents, and because they provide an available subject pool, most research on the neuro-
toxic effects of solvent exposure has been conducted with persons in occupational settings.

sive effects on the central nervous system (CNS). Solvents are lipophilic or strongly attracted to
fats and have an affinity for lipid-rich tissues, including brain tissue, where they have been
implicated in producing a wide range of neurobehavioral symptoms. Highly concentrated acute
exposure can result in symptoms that range from lightheadedness to death. Animal and human
studies have documented these effects (Kulig, 1990).

However, the long-term effects of prolonged, low-level exposure to solvents are contro-
versial. Some studies have purported to document chronic effects in persons whose occupation
brings them into daily contact with solvents (Arlien–Soborg, Bruhn, Gyldensted, & Melgaard,
1979; Lindstrom, 1973; Mikkelsen, Jorgensen, Browne, & Gyldensted, 1988). Other studies
have failed to show such effects (Maizlish et al., 1985; Spurgeon, Glass, Calvert, Cunningham–
Hill, & Harrington, 1994; Triebig et al., 1988), or have reanalyzed data from earlier studies and
refuted claims of detrimental effects from sustained low-grade exposure (Gade, Mortensen, &
Bruhn, 1988; Grasso, Sharratt, Davies, & Irvine, 1984).

evidence from over 20 years of research favors the view that prolonged exposure to organic
solvents causes the chronic and often irreversible neurobehavioral deficits labeled chronic toxic
encephalopathy (Baker, 1994). However, a careful examination of the methodology of the
studies commonly cited as a basis for this conclusion reveals methodological flaws sufficiently

The purpose of this article is to examine some of the methodological flaws of studies
supporting this conclusion. We argue that potential confounds and other factors preclude a
consensus on the chronic neurobehavioral effects of exposure to organic solvents because of
selection bias in recruitment of research subjects; overreliance on subject recall in determin-
ing levels and duration of exposure, assessment of health status and premorbid func-
tioning; between-study variability in tests used to assess neurobehavioral functioning and in the
results; between-study variability in the kinds of solvents examined; and lack of dose–response

Many of the earlier studies were conducted in Scandinavian countries where a “painters’ syn-
drome” could lead to premature retirement and disability benefits. Painters and persons in other
trades would present themselves to clinics with symptoms of “organic solvent disease,” char-
acterized primarily by impairment of memory, lack of coordination, and some deterioration of
personality. The concern of these earlier studies was to document empirically the link between
exposure to organic solvents and behavioral deficits. One specific goal was to assemble a
battery of neuropsychological tests sensitive enough to detect reliably subclinical neurobehav-
ioral effects of exposure to neurotoxins (Hanninen, 1971; Ryan, Morrow, Bromet, & Parkinson,
1987; Russell, Flattau, & Pope, 1990). It was thought that a reliable test battery would be useful
for detecting problems early on before acute effects became chronic, providing a taxonomy of
neurotoxic symptoms, identifying individuals with legitimate complaints, providing guidance
to industry in adopting safety measures whereby employees could avoid overexposure, devel-
oping alternatives to solvents that were less toxic, and so on.
In one study (Gregersen, Mikkelsen, Klausen, Dossing, Neilsen, & Thygesen, 1978), a
“chronic cerebral painters’ syndrome” was reportedly found among a group of 35 painters
ranging in age from 27 to 58 years. A critical review of this research reported several errors in
methodology casting doubt on this finding (Errebo–Knudsen, & Olsen, 1986). Included in the
weakness was lack of a control group matched to the exposed group on potentially confounding
variables, factors related to the same kinds of symptoms or health problems as those linked
to solvents (e.g., alcohol use, health status, educational level, age). Absence of nonexposed
control groups matched to exposed individuals on critical variables has been a repeated criti-
Journal of Clinical Psychology, November 1997
cism of research purporting to find a link between organic solvent exposure and neurobehav-
ioral dysfunction. There are also other factors that are as serious a threat to the validity of these
studies as lack of a control group.
In cross-sectional studies that compared neuropsychological test scores of exposed and nonex-
posed individuals, subjects presumably exposed to organic solvents were selected mostly from
those presenting themselves for assessment and treatment of neuropsychological symptoms.
From the population of individuals with organic solvent exposure, this method sampled only
those with symptoms. Because those with symptoms were overrepresented in samples of exposed
persons, it is not surprising that many studies found reliable differences between exposed and
nonexposed individuals on tests of psychological and neurobehavioral functioning. Unfortu-
nately, this selection method creates apparent differences even when no true relationship exists
between the hypothesized cause and effects.
Linz and colleagues (1986), for example, selected a sample of industrial painters who were
seen at an occupational health clinic for shortness of breath, disorientation, nausea, irritability,
and other symptoms. These individuals were given a number of neuropsychological tests, and
their scores were compared to average normative group scores. Results showed that exposed
individuals scored one or more standard deviations below the normative mean on a number of
neurobehavioral functions including simple motor speed, hand strength, and auditory sensitiv-
ity. Compared to a sample of nonexposed individuals, the personality profile of painters revealed
elevations in depression, anxiety, and preoccupation with somatic concerns.
Morrow et al. (1989) selected subjects with a history of exposure to solvents from among
referrals to an occupational medicine clinic. Their MMPI scores revealed increased elevations
on a number of scales with response profiles indicating a high rate of somatic disturbances,
anxiety, depression, social isolation, and fear of losing control. Additionally, the exposed group
revealed clinical profiles similar to those of former prisoners of war with posttraumatic stress
disorder. In both of these examples, there was no random selection of subjects from the popu-
lation of painters; only those with symptoms were used for comparison with nonexposed samples.
In several follow-up studies, the same selection procedure was present. Juntunen et al.
(1982) selected patients diagnosed as having chronic intoxication caused by exposure to sol-
vents. These patients were evaluated on average 5 years after initial diagnosis. At time of
follow-up, subjective signs of impairment tended to diminish (e.g., headache, tiredness) whereas
clinical signs including cerebellar functions, gait and station, and fine motorics showed deteri-
oration. Bruhn and colleagues (1981) also selected for follow-up house painters diagnosed with
chronic toxic encephalopathy.At time of reassessment (2 years), subjects reported slight improve-
ments in headache and dizziness but either stability or some deterioration in neuropsycholog-
ical functioning. In other studies of various designs, exposed individuals were selected from
those either receiving disability benefits, presenting with various symptoms, or diagnosed with
chronic toxic encephalopathy or some other “painters’ syndrome” (Arlien–Soborg, Henriksen,
Gade, Gyldensted, & Paulson, 1982; Callender, Morrow, Subramanian, Duhon, & Ristovv,
1993; Flodin, Edling, & Axelson, 1984; Gregersen, Klausen, & Elsnab, 1987; Juntunen, Hupli,
Hernberg, & Luisto, 1980; Kelafant, Berg, & Schleenbaker, 1994; Lindstrom, 1973, 1980;
Lindstrom, Antti–Poika, Tola, & Hyytiainen, 1982; Ryan, Morrow, & Hodgson, 1988; van Vliet
Researchers have often attempted to control for factors other than solvent exposure that
could account for symptoms. These competing etiologic factors included alcohol consumption,
use of medication or drugs, preexisting psychological or neurobehavioral dysfunction, general
health problems, life stress, involvement in litigation, work history, and so on. In a follow-up
study by Arlien–Soborg and colleagues (1982), for example, persons exceeding a predeter-
mined level of alcohol consumption, having a history of significant head injury, epilepsy, peri-
natal trauma, encephalitis, or meningitis, were excluded from a solvent exposed sample. Bleecker
and colleagues (1991) attempted to control statistically for a number of potentially confound-
ing factors including alcohol intake and smoking. Husman (1980) controlled for alcohol intake
by matching exposed and referent groups on this variable. Linz et al. (1986) sought to control
competing factors such as unemployment, financial stresses, and pending litigation by includ-
ing a comparison group with similar stressors. In a case-controlled study, van Vliet and col-
leagues (1989) matched painters with construction workers on awards of disability benefits due
to neuropsychiatric disorders.
One problem with attempts to control for these confounds is that self-reports were often
the sole source of information on subjects’ alcohol intake, premorbid functioning, health status,
and the like. A substantial body of research demonstrates the need to be cautious regarding the
accuracy of clinically important information based on unassisted subjective recall (e.g., see
Fiske & Taylor, 1991; Mittenberg, DiGiulio, Perrin, & Bass, 1992). Caution is especially war-
ranted when external incentives, such as disability awards, retirement benefits, escape from
noxious work, or compensation may influence the recall from memory of autobiographical
When attempting to confirm relations between solvent exposure and neurobehavioral function-
ing, establishing the level and duration of exposure to particular solvents is critically important
(Baker, 1988; Baker & White, 1985; Fidler, Baker, & Letz, 1987; Russell et al., 1990). To link
such exposure to behavioral deficits, quantitative evidence of exposure must be established;
inability to do so prevents any but the most crude dose-response characterizations. The ideal, of
course, is precise measurements of intensity and duration of exposure by continuous monitor-
ing. Because this is unrealistic, investigators have relied on secondary sources to estimate these
To estimate exposure, many studies relied on retrospective assessments of workers and
employers regarding occupational history, work environment, job duties, estimates of daily
exposure, quantities of materials used, episodes of acute exposure, and so forth. To estimate
exposure, researchers have used various methods including data from questions on working
conditions and exposure to create an index characterizing exposure (Gregersen et al., 1987);
subjects’ own responses on pension applications (Lindstrom, Riihimaki, & Hanninen, 1984);
semi-structured occupational and environmental chemical exposure questionnaire (Morrow,
Robin, Hodgson, & Kamis, 1992); self-reported estimates of number of hours worked per week
using solvents (Rasmussen, Jeppesen, & Sabros, 1993); detailed, self-reported lifetime job
history questionnaire (Callender et al., 1993); occupational history including working materi-
als, method, and conditions (Gregersen et al., 1987); history of exposure provided by the sub-
ject, the employer, or by occasional hygienic measurements in the workplace (Lindstrom, 1973);
number of years working around solvents (Arlien–Soborg et al., 1979); description of working
conditions provided by worker and by employer, inspection of workplace, environmental mea-
surements, and biological tests (Juntunen, Antti–Poika, Tola, & Partanen, 1982); and inter-
views (Ryan, Morrow, & Hodgson, 1988). Some studies have used biological monitoring of
blood, urine, and exhaled breath to quantify absorption of selected substances (Lauwerys,
1983), but these methods generally are useful only in evaluating recent exposure.
Using interviews to gather data on exposure is especially problematic when the interviewer
is not blind to hypotheses of the study. Expectations regarding findings—for example, that a
Journal of Clinical Psychology, November 1997
group of painters with certain symptoms are likely to have been exposed to X concentration of
solvent for Y period of time—may lead interviewers to behave towards subjects in ways that
yield the expected finding, regardless of its objective accuracy. Self-fulfilling prophecy has
been documented in numerous social psychological studies (Andersen & Bem, 1981; Fazio,
Effrein, & Falender, 1982; Rosenthal & Jacobson, 1968; Snyder & Swann, 1978; Zanna &
Pack, 1975). Faust, Ziskin, and Hiers (1991) commented on the reliability of the clinical inter-
view, but the same criticisms often apply to interviews used in research studies:
Various problems plague the use of interviews. First, numerous extraneous influences, such as the place and
time of examination, influence or distort the data that the clinician obtains. Second, the clinician is far from
a perfect data-gathering instrument and rather is selective or biased in the information gathered and observed.
Third, the interpretation of interview data may be shaped considerably by the clinicians’ theoretical orien-
tation, values and biases. (p. 13)
For the most part researchers have acknowledged the limitations of self-report methods for
obtaining precise measurements of solvent exposure. Some investigators have attempted to
remedy this situation. For example, Elofsson and colleagues (1980) supplemented interviews
of car and industrial spray painters with construction of a model workshop to assess intensity
and duration of long-term exposure. In addition to taking measurements of airborne concen-
trations of solvents present at the time of the study, the reconstructed model workshop appears
likely to have allowed researchers to estimate on-the-job conditions more accurately than with
interviews. Fidler et al. (1987) devised an index of long-term exposure by taking into account
factors modifying construction painters’ intake of solvents including respirator use, method of
paint application, and presence of ventilation. In our view, Spurgeon et al. (1994) were among
those coming closest to the ideal methodology for assessing exposure. Current and past expo-
sure of paintmakers was estimated using extensive data from manufacturing plants where reg-
ular hygiene monitoring had been carried out for many years. These researchers did not find
impairments in cognitive functioning or mental health in the paintmakers. However, aside from
these efforts, most published studies have relied heavily on subjective recall to measure each
The problem with using recall is that information retrieved from memory can be biased
in many ways (Fiske & Taylor, 1991). First, psychological research shows that people seek
information that confirms their beliefs about themselves (Swann, 1983). They see more self-
confirmatory information than may actually exist (Swann, 1987), attend more to self-confir-
matory feedback, and recall preferentially information that is consistent with beliefs about
themselves (Swann & Read, 1981). Additionally, information recalled from memory that is
ambiguous may be interpreted as supportive of current beliefs (Anderson & Pichert, 1978;
Cantor & Mischel, 1977; Hastie, 1981; Loftus, Miller, & Burns, 1978; Snyder & Uranowitz,
1978; Taylor & Crocker, 1981) and autobiographical histories may be rewritten to fit present
This means that patients presenting themselves to clinics for assessment of symptoms
believed to be caused by exposure to solvents can be primed to recall and interpret information
that supports such beliefs. Memories regarding not only exposure history but also prior psy-
chological and cognitive functioning, alcohol intake, lifestyle, and other information necessary
for aiding in assessment of neurotoxic effects, may be distorted by beliefs that one’s symptoms
were caused by exposure to solvents. External incentives such as disability benefits contribute
to biased recall and biased interpretation of information.
To be consistent with beliefs that exposure to solvents caused present symptoms, patients
may recall exposure to solvents in greater concentrations than was the case. Length of exposure
may be overestimated and use of protective equipment underestimated. These reconstructed
estimates may be especially biased if actual exposure limits are uncertain or ambiguous. Evi-
dence regarding overexposure may be buttressed by recall that exaggerates working conditions.
For example, a person may erroneously remember working in a less ventilated environment
than actually existed, thereby enhancing exposure to greater concentrations of hazardous mate-
rial. Recall of physical exertion may be exaggerated, hence increasing intake of airborne mate-
rial through aerobic breathing.
In addition to biased recall of the parameters of exposure, recall of etiologic factors that
compete with solvent exposure may be underestimated. For example, frequency and dose of
alcohol and medication intake may be downplayed, and levels of premorbid intellectual and
sexual functioning elevated, because they are consistent with beliefs that current symptoms
were caused by exposure to solvents. Mittenberg and colleagues (1992), for example, found
that patients reporting similar symptoms underestimated preinjury base rates (using a sample of
traumatic brain injury patients). In sum, studies relying more on self-reports than on objective
information to estimate levels of exposure and the role of competing etiologic factors must be
EXPOSURE TO MIXTURES OF SOLVENTS
Although in the literature and in lectures organic solvents are typically discussed as a group
sharing common properties, between-study comparisons of results are difficult because the
persons studied were not exposed to the same solvents or mixture of solvents. The ability to
compare earlier with later studies is critical in establishing a track record of results for partic-
ular solvents, in making replication studies feasible, and in building a reliable data base from
which a clear understanding of the chronic effects of exposure, if any, can emerge. For exam-
ple, Bruhn et al. (1981) studied white spirit, Spurgeon et al. (1994) and Elofsson et al. (1981)
and Lindstrom (1980) studied mixed solvents, and Morrow (1989) and Linz et al. (1986) did
not specify what they were studying. In most studies, subjects were exposed to a mixture of
solvents rather than to a single solvent, but the amounts and types of solvents involved are
highly variable. This complicates interpretation of findings because different substances have
different CNS effects—untangling the effects of a single solvent would be impossible in these
studies (nor was this the intent of the authors in most cases). Also, potential interaction effects
from exposure to mixtures of solvents need to be considered.
While acknowledging that each has unique effects, most researchers characterize solvents
(implicitly or explicitly) as having similar general effects:
There appear to be both a general diffuse behavioral dysfunction caused by all neurotoxic exposures, or
most of them, and more specific impairments that may be agent-dependent and related to the different
sensitivity of various brain structures to the agent in question. (Anger, 1990a, p. 48)
What these general diffuse effects are is unclear, and how they are distinguished from more
specific effects is unspecified. Other researchers have raised questions about the consistency of
chronic neurological dysfunction because of exposure to various solvents (Spencer & Schaum-
Exposure to solvent mixtures is of particular concern since existing threshold limit values (TLVs) for
individual agents have been set on the assumption that exposure occurs only to one solvent at a time.
Not only do threshold limits vary for different solvents, but reported neurological effects
differ as well. In addition to apparent physical manifestations such as gastrointestinal, cardio-
vascular, and endocrinological dysfunction, carbon disulfide, for example, has nervous-system
effects, including psychosis and peripheral neuropathy (Seppalainen & Tolonen, 1974). The
Journal of Clinical Psychology, November 1997
neurological effects of n-Hexane and other alkanes appear to be confined to sensorimotor or
motor peripheral neuropathy. Trichloroethylene and perchloroethylene are reported to produce
a host of neuropsychiatric effects including tremor, alcohol intolerance, anxiety, bradycardia,
and insomnia (Lilis, Stanescu, Muica, & Roventa, 1969). Further, negative effects from expo-
sure to perchloroethylene are suggested as including changes in personality and recent memory
(Gold, 1969). Early signs from chronic exposure to toluene are reported to include high-
frequency hearing loss, hand tremor, and slurred speech.
Spencer and Schaumburg (1985) observe that “Chronic neurological dysfunction is usually
associated with structural changes in nervous tissue, and the type of damage is often specific
for individual compounds.” (p. 59) Given the diversity of reported neurobehavioral and psy-
chological effects, both acute and chronic, characterizations of the cumulative weight of research
as showing specific identifiable chronic effects from exposure to organic solvents, are pre-
mature. This is not to imply that precautions should not be taken to reduce overexposure to
these materials in the workplace. Uncertainty with regard to chronic effects should not deter
industry from taking steps to protect employees. However, expert claims of ability to identify
with reasonable certainty specific effects of chronic or repeated, low-level exposure to solvents
VARIATION IN TESTS AND FINDINGS
Paralleling the problem of how to interpret mixed studies that examine different solvents,
comparisons are further complicated because of between-study variation in assessment instru-
ments and results. Anger (1990b) reported that 250 tests were used in 185 studies of various
workplace neurotoxicants. Because of this diversity, a data base of consistent, reliable findings
is lacking. As with comparing different solvents, variation in assessment procedures and find-
ings makes problematic comparisons of earlier with later studies. Indeed, one component of
this problem, namely test selection, has been a major concern to researchers (Amler, Lybarger,
Anger, Phifer, Chappell, & Hutchinson, 1994; Anger et al., 1994; Baker et al., 1983; Russell
et al., 1990). In eight studies cited by Hanninen (1990), 38 different tests were used. Uncer-
tainty regarding prototypical symptoms is also reflected in the diversity of labels used to char-
acterize long-term exposure to solvents. Organic brain syndrome, chronic toxic encephalopathy,
chronic organic solvent intoxication, chronic painters’syndrome, presenile syndrome, and psycho-
organic syndrome, among others, have been used as diagnostic categories.
Variation both in test selection and in findings has characterized solvent neurotoxicity
research from the beginning. In an early investigation, Hanninen and colleagues (1976) assessed
the effects of long-term exposure to mixed solvents using, from the Wechsler Adult Intelligence
Scale (WAIS), Similarities, Picture Completion, Block Design, and Digit Span; Logical Mem-
ory from the WMS; Associate Learning; the Benton test for visual reproduction and retention;
Santa Ana Dexterity Test; Finger tapping; Reaction times; choice reaction time; and the Mira
test. Compared to nonexposed controls, exposed car painters showed impairments in verbal and
visual intelligence, and in verbal memory.
Morrow and colleagues (1992) tested exposed persons using the Pittsburgh Occupational
Exposures Test Battery (POET; Morrow, Robin, Hodgson, & Kamis, 1992) consisting of WAIS
Subtests, WMS Visual Reproduction, Verbal Associative Learning, Symbol-Digit Learning,
Incidental Memory, Recurring Words, Boston Embedded Figures, Mental Rotation, Trail Mak-
ing Test, and Grooved Pegboard. Results from Experiment 1 showed that in comparison to
controls, exposed subjects: recalled significantly less information on measures of verbal and
symbol–digit-paired associate learning, but there were no reliable differences following a delay;
recalled less information on Logical Memories Immediate and Delayed Recall, again with no
differences in percent of information recalled following a delay; and exhibited differences for
all intervals on the Four-Word Short-Term Memory Test.
Lindstrom (1980) compared the performance of an exposed group to controls on subtests
of the Wechsler Adult Intelligence Scale (similarities, digit span, digit symbol, picture comple-
tion, and block design), and on other performance tests such as Visual Memory, Benton Visual
Retention, Symmetry Drawing, Santa Ana Dexterity, and the Mira. Declines in visuomotor
performance and decreased freedom from distractibility characterized the solvent-exposed group.
A relation was found between duration of solvent exposure and poor visuomotor performances.
Maroni and colleagues (1977) used a psychological test battery including Raven Progres-
sive Matrices, Picture Completion and Block Design from the WAIS, Pauli test and Symbol–
Number Association to test for differences between exposed and nonexposed female factory
workers. No significant differences were observed between these groups.
Spurgeon et al. (1994) used the Neurobehavioral Evaluation System (NES; Baker & Letz,
1986), a computerized neuropsychological battery that included paired associate learning, hand-
eye coordination, symbol–digit substitution, digit–span, pattern memory, continuous perfor-
mance, colour word vigilance, and associate recall. No dose-effect relations were found for
paintmakers exposed to varying levels of solvent. There were, however, differences between
paintmakers and controls on the continuous performance and color word vigilance tests for
persons in medium- and high-exposure groups.
Acknowledging the lack of consistency in previous studies, especially with regard to test
selection that tended to be idiosyncratic (Hanninen, 1990), several workshops convened to
develop systematic strategies for investigating the chronic effects of long-term, occupational
and residential exposure to organic solvents. Among these were the Workshop on Neurobehav-
ioral Testing convened by the Agency for Toxic Substances and Disease Registry in 1991 and
the World Health Organization meeting in 1985. Unfortunately, the recommendations for test
batteries produced by these workshops are relatively recent, and there is not yet a generally
accepted data base. That the workshops were convened is acknowledgment of the problem.
Notwithstanding the diversity of psychological functions assessed, variability in assess-
ment tools and procedures, and differences in findings, some investigators reasoned that a core
set of functions showing consistent neurotoxic effects could be gleaned from prior research.
For example, after reviewing existing literature on neurobehavioral testing in solvent-exposed
populations, Baker and White (1985) felt they had observed a pattern of neuropsychological
symptoms. They reported that, overall, short-term memory and psychomotor function were
poorer in solvent-exposed than in control subjects. In a later article, 16 studies published since
1985 were cited as supporting this conclusion. Baker (1994) argued that, in the aggregate,
chronic solvent exposure causes neurobehavioral dysfunction in these functional domains.
Difficulties with neuropsychological testing are not confined to their application in assess-
ing the consequences of exposure to organic solvents. Neuropsychological testing itself is not
without controversy. Commenting on this, Faust, Ziskin, & Hiers (1991) state,
There is considerable diversity in approaches to neuropsychological assessment. Wasyliw and Golden (1985)
note that due to the variation in method of neuropsychological evaluation it is nearly impossible to provide
a standard description. Indeed, differences in examination techniques can be so broad that two neuropsy-
chologists examining the same person may employ tests with absolutely no overlap in specific content.
These authors comment further:
. . . there is not a majority group within neuropsychology—there is no specific set of measures or tests
utilized by most practitioners. This tremendous diversity likely reflects the ongoing controversies and dis-
putes within neuropsychology, and the lack of agreement about standards or methods of assessment. (p. 21)
Journal of Clinical Psychology, November 1997
The conclusion that long-term, low-level exposure to organic solvents causes a host of chronic
psychological and neuropsychological symptoms has been based on studies with such serious
flaws that this hypothesis cannot reasonably be considered confirmed. Because of selection
bias in recruitment of research subjects, overreliance on subjective recall in determining levels
and duration of exposure, between-study variability in solvents examined and in tests used to
assess neurobehavioral functioning, and differences in reported findings, unequivocal claims
In cross-sectional studies, persons suspected of exposure to solvents have too often been
selected from medical clinics, from the rolls of persons receiving disability benefits, or from
those diagnosed with solvent-induced organic brain syndrome, chronic toxic encephalopathy,
or painters’ syndrome. By selecting only symptomatic individuals for the experimental group
members, these researchers inadvertently removed from study those exposed persons who were
asymptomatic, thus skewing the exposed sample in the direction of dysfunction. This selective
sampling procedure also deprived us of the protection afforded by the normal practice of ran-
dom sampling—protection which is especially important with settings and samples associated
with relevant response biases, which is generally the case in this literature.
To establish dose-response relationships, accurate quantitative evidence of exposure to
target materials is essential. In conducting these studies, however, obtaining precise informa-
tion on intensity and duration of exposure is difficult and at times impossible. Consequently,
many studies relied on self-reports of exposure history in establishing these levels. Unassisted
recall is so commonly unreliable, especially in settings which foster response biases, that these
data cannot be regarded as valid measurements. If symptomatic patients believe they know the
cause of those symptoms, they are likely to selectively retrieve from memory information
supporting such beliefs, thereby providing researchers with misleading or inaccurate data.
Between-study variability in the kinds and combinations of solvents examined contributes
to uncertainty regarding the long-term effects of exposure. The majority of exposures involve
multiple solvents rather than a single solvent and sometimes simultaneous exposure to materi-
als other than solvents that are neurotoxicants (e.g., lead). Because each solvent has unique
neurobehavioral effects, distinguishing the consequences of exposure to one solvent from another,
and determining interaction effects, remains partially speculative. These complications make
between-study comparisons difficult and hinder construction of a reliable data base on the
neurobehavioral consequences of exposure to particular solvents.
Because the instruments employed in this literature have not been correlated and cross-
validated, between-study variation in assessment instruments makes it speculative to treat super-
ficially similar findings as confirmatory. Many tests with similar names are not measuring
identical parameters, based on their low intercorrelations and contradictory outcomes. One
researcher reported that in 185 studies exploring workplace exposure to chemicals (e.g., sol-
vents, mercury, lead), 250 different tests were administered (Anger, 1990b). Additionally, dif-
ferent results have been reported from one study to the next—often contradictory results. Many
studies have reported psychological and neuropsychological deficits, but the deficits reported
are inconsistent. Others have not found these deficits. Studies that have reported deficits often
differ in the kind and intensity of dysfunction. Differential assessment methods and findings
retard establishment of a track record of results and complicate replication studies.
To assist investigators in developing research methods that help minimize many of the
problems outlined here, we suggest employing two strategies: random sampling of research
participants and use of emerging computerized neurobehavioral testing (e.g., Amler, Anger, &
Sizemore, 1995; Baker, Letz, & Fidler, 1985; Letz, 1991). Random sampling increases the
probability that each member of a particular population (e.g., house painters, degreasers) rather
than a subset of the population (house painters or degreasers with symptoms or in litigation)
has an equal chance of participating in neurobehavioral testing.
By selecting samples at random from the entire population rather than from those visiting
health clinics, applying for disability, pursuing litigation, and so on, some of the major flaws in
the existing research will be corrected. For example, the skewing of results in either direction—in
favor of pathology or not—are assuaged because research participants with and without symp-
toms are randomly distributed in the sample. Biased recall of dosage (i.e., intensity and dura-
tion of chemical exposure) and estimates of variables that compete with chemical exposure in
explaining symptoms can be minimized by random sampling. For example, the influence of
overestimates of “premorbid” intellectual functioning and exposure levels, and underestimates
of alcohol use, life stressors, prior health problems, and so forth, are minimized because par-
ticipants with incentives to inflate exposure levels or to deflate alternative causes of symptoms
(e.g., participants in litigation) are not overrepresented in the sample. Along similar lines, we
urge the adoption of a policy in scientific journals requiring researchers to warn the reader
when samples include plaintiffs or other individuals with obvious potential for response biases.
Use of computerized neurobehavioral testing procedures may permit standardization of
procedures at the levels of administration and scoring. In short, computerized testing increases
the likelihood that researchers will be conducting research in a similar manner with similar
instruments, thus permitting rigorous replication of research. In this way, a more reliable data
base regarding the neurobehavioral consequences of low-dose exposure to organic solvents can
emerge. An additional advantage of computerized testing is that inadvertent researcher influ-
ences can be held to a minimum (see e.g. Campbell, 1993; Rosenthal, 1993).
In sum, the confluence of methodological weaknesses reviewed here is sufficient to war-
rant skepticism of the claim that the weight of research evidence unequivocally supports the
hypothesis that there exists a causal link between repeated, low-level exposure to solvents and
lasting neurobehavioral dysfunction. We recognize the difficulty of researching this area, but
until methodological issues like those outlined here are addressed adequately in the future,
uncertainty will continue to cloud this important topic.
